column3: In the eighteenth year of his reign, Ptolemy Philometor granted to Onias, who was the son of Onias the holy Pontiff, permission to build a temple at Heliopolis in Egypt, similar to that of Jerusalem, which was called by him Onion; it stood for 233 years, and was finally destroyed together with the temple of Jerusalem by Titus and Vespasian. So Josephus, Eusebius, and others. At length Philometor, craftily wishing to deprive his son-in-law Alexander of the kingdom of Asia, died suddenly in the year of the Greeks 167. 1 Maccabees 11, vv. 1 and 18.

Ptolemy Physcon, or Euergetes, brother of Philometor, reigned after his brother for 29 years. He was by a strange prodigy at once brother, husband, and son-in-law of Cleopatra; for he took her daughter to wife. Moreover, when Cleopatra was reigning alone while Physcon her husband lived in vice, Physcon arranged for both his own son and hers to be cut into pieces, placed in a chest, and set before her at table on her birthday, as Justin attests in books 38 and 39.
Ptolemy Lathurus, son of Ptolemy Euergetes, surnamed by others Soter, reigned for 16 years.
Ptolemy Alexander, brother of Lathurus, reigned jointly with his mother, who had expelled Lathurus, for 10 years.
Ptolemy Lathurus, restored to the kingdom on account of his mother's cruelty, reigned again for another 8 years.

Ptolemy called Auletes, because he had practiced playing the flute, son of Lathurus, according to Salianus. He begot Cleopatra, who was the last queen of Egypt. He reigned in total together with his daughter Berenice for 30 years.
Ptolemy Dionysius, son of Auletes, since he was a minor, ruled Egypt through his sister Cleopatra for 5 years.

Cleopatra, having overthrown her brother Ptolemy Dionysius, reigned alone for 17 years. She is that celebrated concubine of Mark Antony the triumvir, who was defeated in battle by Augustus Caesar and perished; Cleopatra, lest she should fall into the hands of Augustus, killed herself by the bite of asps. Augustus then subjugated all Egypt to himself and the Romans, and thus began to be monarch in the year of the city 724, which was the third year of Olympiad 187. All these matters are treated by Eusebius, Justin, Saint Epiphanius, Bede, Salianus, Torniellus, and others. These things will shed great light on both books of Maccabees. For the kings Antiochus and Demetrius of Asia long contended with the Ptolemaic kings of Egypt over Judaea, indeed over empire itself; for Judaea lay between them both, and was therefore for both a bone of contention, as each strove to claim it for himself.

A chronological arrangement of the Maccabees, or Asmoneans, who governed the people not only as Pontiffs but also as Princes. 1. Mattathias, rising up against Antiochus Epiphanes in the ninth year of Epiphanes, which was the year of the Greeks 145, held the Pontificate and Principate of Israel for a few months. 1 Maccabees 2, v. 1.

2. Judas Maccabaeus succeeded his father Mattathias at his death, as if the firstborn son, in the Pontificate and Principate; he defended his homeland and the true worship of God most bravely and successfully; he purified the temple, having expelled the idol of Antiochus, in the year of the Greeks 146; at last he was slain fighting most fiercely against Bacchides in the year of the Greeks 152, 1 Maccabees 9, vv. 6 and 18. He ruled and defended Israel for 6 years.

3. Jonathan was appointed by the people to succeed his brother Judas, later approved by Alexander Balas, son of Antiochus Epiphanes, 1 Maccabees 10, vv. 5 and 20. At last he was treacherously killed by Tryphon in the year of the Greeks 170, 1 Maccabees 16, vv. 23 and 24, having ruled for 19 years.

4. Simon was substituted for his brother Jonathan in the Dukedom and Pontificate, 1 Maccabees 13, vv. 8 and 25. He was treacherously killed by his son-in-law Ptolemy in the year of the Greeks 177, 1 Maccabees last chapter, last verse, having governed for 7 years.

5. John Hyrcanus, son of Simon, served as leader and Pontiff for 31 years. He is mentioned in the last chapter of 1 Maccabees, last verse, and with him ends the sacred history of the Maccabees, and indeed all the history of the Old Testament and Holy Scripture.

6. Aristobulus, son of John Hyrcanus. He was the first to assume the title and crown of king, which his descendants thereafter followed his example in assuming. He slew his brother Antigonus, and in that same year, by God's vengeance, died vomiting blood. He reigned for 1 year.

7. Jannaeus, the younger brother of Aristobulus, who, to please the Greeks, assumed a Greek name and was called Alexander. He left two sons, Hyrcanus and Aristobulus, who contended with each other over the kingdom all the way to Herod, and were the ruin of it. He reigned for 27 years.
8. Alexandra, wife of Jannaeus, upon his death usurping the kingdom, reigned for 9 years.

9. Aristobulus the younger, son of Jannaeus, excluding his elder brother Hyrcanus, seized the Pontificate and kingdom; but he was captured by Pompey and sent to Rome with his two sons Antigonus and Alexander, having reigned for 3 years. Then Pompey took Jerusalem and subjected all Judaea to Roman rule and made it tributary, during the consulship of Cicero and Antonius.
10. Hyrcanus the elder, son of Jannaeus, was restored to the Pontificate by Pompey, and tossed by various vicissitudes held it for 22 years.

11. Alexander, son of Aristobulus, having escaped from Rome, attempted to seize Judaea; but after three years was captured by Gabinius and beheaded by Pompey; his father Aristobulus himself also, fleeing from Rome with his other son Antigonus, seized Judaea and the Pontificate with the help of the Parthians for three and a half years. But Julius Caesar adjudged the Pontificate to Hyrcanus, as Pompey had already done before, and entrusted to Antipater the guardianship of Idumaea, Judaea, and Hyrcanus, as it were as procurator; then Mark Antony made Herod and Phasael, sons of Antipater, tetrarchs. Finally Antigonus, the last of the Maccabees, at Herod's request was beheaded after some years. With Antigonus the entire stock of the Asmoneans came to an end, as did their kingdom and Pontificate, since they had held both for 126 years according to Josephus, or rather for 130 years according to Salianus and others. For so many years elapsed from the ninth year of Antiochus Epiphanes to the first year of Herod, in which he himself, having slain Antigonus, occupied the kingdom with full right, and in him the scepter departed from Judah, and therefore Christ came according to the oracle of Jacob, Genesis 49:10. All these things are clear from Josephus, Eusebius, Salianus, Torniellus, Pererius, and others.

A series of Pontiffs from Alexander the Great to Christ. 1. Jaddus. He went out to meet Alexander the Great and reconciled him, who was hostile to the Jews, as Josephus attests in books 11 and 13 near the end. He served, according to Eusebius, for 17 years. The younger brother of Jaddus was Manasses, who went to the temple built by his father-in-law Sanballat on Mount Gerizim, similar to that of Jerusalem, and became its Pontiff; concerning him there was perpetual strife and contention between the Jews and the Samaritans all the way to Christ, as is clear from John 4. See Josephus, book 11, chapter 7.
2. Onias the first, son of Jaddus, to whom some assign 23 years.
3. Simon, son of Onias, surnamed the Just, whose praises are described in Ecclesiasticus chapter 50, v. 1.
4. Eleazar, brother of Simon, who sent 72 interpreters to Ptolemy Philadelphus to translate the Old Testament from Hebrew into Greek.
5. Manasses, son of the first Onias, brother of Simon, and uncle of Eleazar.
6. Onias the second, son of Simon the Just, surnamed the Miser.
7. Simon the second, son of Onias the second.
8. Onias the third, surnamed the Holy, son of Simon the second, who by his prayers on behalf of the scourged Heliodorus...

And it came to pass, after Alexander (the Great), son of Philip the Macedonian, who previously (Greek: πρότερον, that is, 'before') reigned in Greece — namely, over all of it, as Serarius says — "had gone forth from the land of Cethim," that is, from the overseas regions of Judaea (for the Jews call these Cethim), such as Macedonia, Greece, Cyprus, etc. Just as today the Italians call those who dwell beyond the Alps 'ultramontanes.' Again, some take Cethim to mean properly Macedonia (which is today called Albania), both because Philip and the Persians are called kings of the Cethaeans, that is, of the Macedonians, in 1 Macc. 8:5; and because Suidas, under the words Latini and Κιτιεῖς, asserts that Macedonia is properly so called from Cethim, namely Κίτιοι, and in the Attic form Μακεδόν. So also St. Jerome, in Book 5 on Isaiah ch. 23, and on ch. 27 of Ezekiel, and on Daniel ch. 11, takes Cethim to mean the islands of Macedonia; and St. Epiphanius, Heresy 30, says Alexander is called one who went forth from Cethim, that is from Macedonia, because there was in it the stock of the Cyprians and Rhodians, to whom this name properly belongs: Cethim.
He struck the city suddenly, and smote it with a great plague, and destroyed many people from Israel.
And he took the spoils of the city, and set fire to it, and pulled down its houses and its walls round about.
And they led away captive the women and the children, and the cattle they possessed.
And they built the city of David with a great and strong wall, and with strong towers, and it became their citadel.
And they placed there a sinful nation, wicked men, and they fortified themselves therein, and they stored up arms and provisions, and gathered together the spoils of Jerusalem.
And they laid them up there, and became a great snare.
And this served as an ambush against the sanctuary, and as an evil adversary to Israel at all times.
And they shed innocent blood round about the sanctuary, and defiled the sanctuary.
And the inhabitants of Jerusalem fled because of them, and the city became a dwelling of strangers, and became a stranger to its own offspring, and her children forsook her.
Her sanctuary was desolate like a wilderness, her feast days were turned into mourning, her sabbaths into reproach, her honours into nothing.
According to her glory, so was her dishonour multiplied, and her excellency was turned into mourning.
And king Antiochus wrote to all his kingdom, that all the people should be one, and that every one should leave his own law.
And all the Gentiles consented according to the word of king Antiochus.
And many of Israel consented to his service, and they sacrificed to idols, and profaned the sabbath.
And the king sent letters by the hands of messengers to Jerusalem and to all the cities of Judah, that they should follow the laws of the Gentiles of the land;
and that they should forbid holocausts, and sacrifices, and atonements to be made in the temple of God;
and that they should forbid the keeping of the sabbath and the solemn days;
and he commanded the holy things to be profaned, and the holy people of Israel.
And he commanded altars to be built, and temples, and idols, and swine's flesh to be sacrificed, and unclean beasts;
and that they should leave their children uncircumcised, and defile their souls with all uncleannesses and abominations, so that they should forget the law and change all the ordinances of God.
And whosoever would not do according to the word of king Antiochus, should die.
According to all these words he wrote to his whole kingdom, and appointed overseers over the people who should compel these things to be done.
And they commanded the cities of Judah to sacrifice.
And many of the people gathered together to them who had forsaken the law of the Lord, and they committed evils in the land;
and they drove the people of Israel into hiding places, and into the secret places of fugitives.

On the fifteenth day of the month Casleu, in the year one hundred and forty-five, king Antiochus built the abominable idol of desolation upon the altar of God, and throughout all the cities of Judah round about they built altars;
and before the doors of the houses and in the streets they burned incense, and sacrificed.
And the books of the law of God they burned with fire, rending them.
And whosoever was found with the books of the testament of the Lord, and whosoever observed the law of the Lord, according to the edict of the king, they put him to death.
In their power they did these things to the people of Israel who were found in every city month by month.
And on the twenty-fifth day of the month they sacrificed upon the altar which was over against the altar of God.
And the women that circumcised their children were slain according to the commandment of king Antiochus.
And they hanged the children about their necks throughout all their houses, and those who had circumcised them they put to death.
And many of the people of Israel determined with themselves that they would not eat unclean things, and chose rather to die than to be defiled with unclean meats.
And they would not break the holy law of God, and were put to death.
And there was very great wrath upon the people.

Cyprus is there, and from thence any other islands. Moreover the Hebrews call all transmarine regions 'islands,' such as Italy, Greece, Macedonia. This is supported by Livy in Book 5 of Decade 2, who names Citium in Macedonia, to which the whole army had assembled. The affinity of the Macedonian name with Cethim also supports this. Wherefore Dionysius on the situation of the earth and Hesychius in his Lexicon call Macedonia 'Macela' and the Macedonians 'Macetas.' Hence Ausonius in his famous Epigrams 2: 'and to raise the Macedonian name.' Indeed Thucydides Book 2, ch. 2, says that Cyprians migrated into Macedonia, so that they were able to give their name Cethim to it. So also Theod. Annius, Pintus, and Fererius on Isaiah 2 take Cethim to mean Macedonia.

Some believed, he says, that the provinces were distributed by the testament of Alexander; and he asserts that this was handed down by various authors, although he himself holds the contrary opinion. Moreover, to our Author, to whom the Greek and Latin Fathers, indeed the whole Church, attribute sacred authority as a hagiographic writer, greater credence must be given.
nus in Syriaco, Justinus et alii. Quare tunc frendens Antiochus castra movit in vicinos Judaeos, inque eos suam indignationem et furorem effudit.

Et locutus est in superbia magna,] jactans se, quasi esset Deus quidam terrestris, cui omnes obedire deberent, et cui nemo resistere posset, uti faciet ejus antitypus Antichristus. Vide dicta Daniel. 11. 36.

Et speciositas mulierum immutata est,] tum quia ipsae depositis vestibus speciosis in communi luctu lugubres, id est nigras et squalidas induerunt; tum quia timor, moeror et angor faciebat eas pallidas et luridas. Sic in Chaldaeorum clade ait Jerem. Thren. 1. de virginibus: Egressus est a filia Sion omnis decor ejus; de adolescentibus c. 4. versu 2: Filii Sion inclyti, et amicti auro primo, quomodo reputati sunt in vasa testea; et v. 7. de Nazaraeis: Candidiores Nazaraei nive, nitidiores lacte, rubicundiores ebore antiquo, sapphiro pulchriores; denigrata est super carbones facies eorum, et non sunt cogniti in plateis.
Et commota est terra,] Judaea perculsa et concussa fuit ingenti terrore, moerore et angore, super habitantes in ea,] scilicet propter incolas Judaeos ab Antiocho occisos, captos et venditos.

Et post duos annos dierum, id est, post duos integros annos omnibus suis diebus plane expletos, ita ut insuper aliquot dies excurrerent, Ant. Epiphanes misit principem tributorum,] quem ipse colligendis tributis praefecerat, nomine Apollonium ad Judaeos non tantum spoliandos, sed et jugulandos, ut dicitur 2. Machab 5. 26.
Succendit eam (Jerosolymam jam a se spoliatam) igni, et detraxit domos et muros ejus in circuitu,] ne Antiocho rebellare posset.
Et aedificaverunt civitatem David,] scilicet arcem Sion, quae toti urbi subjacenti quasi rupes, vel turris dominabatur, muro magno (alto) et firmo.
Et posuerunt illic gentem peccatricem,] scilicet milites rapaces, homicidas et sceleratos, qui Judaeos diriperent, vincirent, mactarent.

Et factum est hoc ad insidias sanctificationi,] id est sanctuario, sive templo, ut scilicet insidiarentur Judaeis euntibus ad templum sacrificii, vel orationis causa, ac erumpentes ex arce in eos irruerent, eosque spoliarent, caperent, vulnerarent. Volebat enim Antiochus abolere Dei cultum, ac Judaeos compellere ad adorandum sua idola, ideoque imaginem Jovis Olympii collocarat in templo.

Lapide notes on Daniel 11:33-35: 'And they that are learned among the people shall teach many; yet they shall fall by the sword, and by flame, and by captivity, and by spoil, for many days.' This refers to the learned who fell under Antiochus, that they might be tried and purified and made white even to the time appointed. God in His great wrath permitted His people to be so grievously afflicted by Antiochus on account of their sins. # GEM

In those days Mattathias went out — or by crasis Matthias, as Josephus and Theodoret call him: which name in Hebrew signifies "gift of God," and is therefore the same as in Greek Theodosius, Theodorus, or inversely Dorotheus, and in Latin Adeodatus; for this man was given by God as a great gift, as a leader of Israel to resist Antiochus. One asks: Who and what manner of man was Mattathias? Answer: he was a priest of the first class from the tribe of Levi, namely from the stock of Joarib, which is placed first among the priestly families, 1 Paral. 24:7. Moreover, this Mattathias, chosen and impelled by God and approved by the people, was made Pontiff and leader and avenger of the people against the common enemy Antiochus; for after the return from captivity by order of Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes, the same persons were Pontiffs and leaders of the people, as Josephus attests in book 11.

[On verse 64: "You therefore, sons, be strengthened and act manfully in the law; for in it you shall be glorious."] And behold Simon your brother — I know he is a man of counsel; hear him always, and he shall be a father to you.
And Judas Maccabaeus, strong in might from his youth, let him be your prince of war, and he shall wage the battle of the people.
And you shall bring to yourselves all the doers of the law, and avenge the vengeance of your people.
Render retribution to the Gentiles and attend to the precept of the law.
And he blessed them and was gathered to his fathers.
And he died in the year one hundred and forty-six; and he was buried by his sons in the sepulchres of his fathers in Modin, and all Israel mourned him with a great mourning.

And he fled, he and his sons] from Jerusalem, where he had been living as a priest, and had slain the Jew sacrificing to idols at the altar; into the mountains among wild beasts living their life, and feeding on wild food, lest they should be partakers of defilement, as is said in the book [that follows].

Third, because the Essenes devoted themselves solely to God through prayer, reading, and contemplation. Hence: "Among them," says Philo in his book on the Essenes, "no one makes swords, spears, helmets, breastplates, shields, nor war-machines, nor anything else useful for war." But the Assideans abounded in these things, being devoted not to the choir, as the Essenes were, but to war. See what I said about the Essenes at Acts 2. I say therefore that the Assideans were soldiers of a holy war, who gave their name to a sacred militia for the sake of the Law and the Synagogue; such as were afterwards the soldiers of the Temple, and as now are the military Orders of the Knights of Malta, of St. John, of St. Stephen, of the Teutons, and similar orders, who fight nobly for the Church of God against Turks, Saracens, and heretics. For when Antiochus was striving to overthrow the Law of God, the Temple, and the Synagogue, God stirred up the Assideans, who devoted themselves to the sacred militia and fought for the protection of the Temple and the Law, offering themselves readily to all the dangers of war. That this is so is clear first from the word "synagogue," that is, assembly, collection, congregation of soldiers for defending the Synagogue. Second, from the word "strong in strength." Third, from the phrase "voluntary in the law," that is, one who voluntarily out of zeal would defend and champion the Law. Therefore the Assideans joined themselves to Judas Maccabaeus as to the leader of the holy war, and were his chief soldiers, through whom he obtained so many and so great victories. This is clear from 2 Macc. 14:6: "Those who are called Assideans among the Jews, over whom Judas Maccabaeus presides, nourish wars and stir up seditions, and do not suffer the kingdom to be at peace." These are the words of enemies, who accused the Assideans as seditious persons before King Demetrius.
The assembly of scribes treats of making common peace with Bacchides and Alcimus openly. Hence it is clear that they were men honored and of the first rank in Israel.

And they smote sinners, that is apostates, who had defected from Judaism to Antiochus and paganism. For this was the supreme sin and the greatest iniquity, by which all religion, faith and virtue was betrayed and destroyed.

With force: supply: they circumcised uncircumcised boys, as many as they found in the borders of Israel. That is, they circumcised boys by force, even against the will of their parents, because the parents were Israelites and thus were obliged to circumcise their sons, and therefore could be compelled to do so by Mattathias as their leader and pontiff, just as the Church now compels the faithful to bring their sons for Baptism. But if there were among them sons of unbelievers, these also they circumcised as now their own and taken by right of war and separated from their parents. And although Lyra says this was done only in the sons of Jews, yet Scripture speaks absolutely; and although Domingo de Soto in Book II on Baptism says: If Mattathias did this with the sons of the Gentiles, he did it indeed according to zeal but not according to knowledge; yet this seems not rightly said of so distinguished and most holy a man: better therefore it is asserted that they, since they were already reduced to servitude by right of war and separated from their parents, could rightly be circumcised, just as we also now rightly baptize sons of Saracens captured in war, says Suarez, 3rd part, vol. 5, q. 67, art. 10, disp. 25, sect. 4.

They obtained the law from the hand of the nations, that is, God's law and worship taken away by Antiochus and the nations they restored to Israel by arms, and caused the law and worship of God to obtain its former dignity and veneration.

Chastisement, etc. and the wrath of indignation, namely of God, with which God, angered and indignant at our sins, chastises us so harshly through Antiochus, loosening for him the reins of raging and being fierce against us. Thus Attila king of the Huns, laying waste Gaul and Italy, when asked by St. Lupus bishop of Troyes who he was and what he wanted, answered that he was the scourge of God for chastising the crimes of the faithful: thus Tamerlane was a scourge of God, and now the Ottomans are. Thus Nebuchadnezzar.

Joseph in the time of his distress (which he suffered in prison) kept the commandment of the fifth of the Decalogue: 'Thou shalt not commit adultery,' because he refused to lie with his master's wife when she solicited him; and therefore he was made Lord of Egypt. Excellently St. Cyprian in his tract on mortality says: 'Adversities are not scandals for you, but battles; nor should they weaken or break the faith of a Christian, but rather show forth virtue in the struggle, since every injury of present evils is to be despised in confidence of future goods. Unless the fight has gone before, there can be no victory; when victory has been won in the encounter of battle, then the crown is given to the victors. The pilot is recognized in the storm, the soldier is proved in the line of battle.' And further: 'Adversities do not call us away from the truth of virtue and faith, but strengthen us in suffering. How great a sublimity it is to stand erect amid the ruins of the human race!' etc. # GEM

Phinehas our father (here it is clear that Mattathias and the Maccabees were descended from the tribe of Levi and from Phinehas the High Priest, and therefore when the others fell away, the High Priesthood belonged to them by right of succession), by being zealous with the zeal of God, by killing the prince who was sacrificing to Baal-peor, received the covenant, that is, God's promise and pact of an eternal priesthood, namely that the High Priesthood would endure in his posterity until the end of the Synagogue and of Judaism, that is until Christ. See the notes on Numbers 25.

Joshua (the successor of Moses), when he fulfilled the word, that is the commandment by which God through Moses ordered him to survey Canaan (Num. 14), to fight against Amalek (Exod. 17), and after Moses to take care of the people (Josh. 1), was made a leader in Israel, succeeding Moses in the leadership of the people.

Caleb, when he testified in the assembly against his fellow spies who said that Canaan was unconquerable on account of its giant inhabitants and fortified cities, saying that with God's help it could be conquered and would in fact be conquered by the Hebrews as God had promised, received an inheritance, a particular one in Hebron (Num. and Josh. 14:12). See the praises of Joshua and Caleb in Ecclus. 46.

David in his mercy, that is through the merit of his mercifulness, by which he was dutiful and merciful both toward others and toward Saul who was persecuting him, refusing to harm him when he had him in his power, obtained the throne of the kingdom for ever, so that he would hold royal power and transmit it to his posterity, to endure for them as long as the kingdom of Israel and Judah endured, namely until the Babylonian captivity. For 'eternal' here does not signify absolute perpetuity but relative and limited perpetuity, namely perpetual duration throughout the entire state and kingdom of Judah, so that as long as this endured, the kingdom of David would endure in it.

Elias, while he was zealous with the zeal of the law, killing all the prophets and priests of Baal and opposing himself to Achab, Jezabel, and the Baalites (3 Kings 18), was taken up into heaven, carried off thence in a fiery chariot (4 Kings 2).
Ananias, Azarias, and Misael, believing in the true God and for this faith offering themselves to the flames, believing that God could rescue them from them, were saved from the flame (Dan. 3).
Daniel in his simplicity, that is in his integrity, candor, and constancy in persisting in the worship of the one true God, was for that merit liberated from the mouth of the lions (Dan. 6).

And from the words of a sinful man (the impious minister of Antiochus) be not afraid, since his glory is dung and worms, so that it may corrupt, putrefy, and stink, when shortly afterwards through God's vengeance all his glory is turned into ignominy and all his fame into infamy, as is clear to have happened to Antiochus in 2 Macc. 9.

You therefore, my sons, be strengthened and act manfully in defending and championing the law, for in it you shall be glorious, both by fighting and conquering, and by dying gloriously for it, as all of them died for it, namely Judas, Jonathan, and Simon. For what is more glorious than to fight and die for the law, for the fatherland, for God? Mattathias encourages his sons to the fierce contests that were threatening them with Antiochus, and therefore sets before them the examples of the heroes of old, so that they might imitate them and attain a glory like theirs; for nothing so encourages one in difficult matters as the examples of ancestors. Hence Ovid (Trist. bk. 4): 'Fill the sad material with your virtues; glory travels by a steep and precipitous path. Who would have known Hector, if Troy had been fortunate? The public road of virtue runs through evils. Your art, Tiphys, lies idle if there are no waves on the sea; if men are well, your art, Phoebus, lies idle. Virtue, which lies hidden and is idle in good times and goes unrecognized, appears and is demonstrated by evils. Use the times whose gift you now enjoy, and a broad arena lies open for your praises.'

And behold Simon your brother, I know that he is a man of counsel; hear him always, and he shall be a father to you, so that he may direct you by his counsels as a father directs his sons, and likewise that he may govern my house and family as a father. Hence Josippus son of Gorion and Annius of Viterbo think that Simon was the firstborn of Mattathias. But Josephus, the Seder Olam Minor, and the Hebrews report that Judas was the firstborn, whence he also succeeded his father in the leadership and High Priesthood; that the second-born was John; that the third was Jonathan; and that the fourth was Simon.

And Judas Maccabaeus, mighty in strength from his youth, shall be the commander of your army. Hence it is clear that Judas not only in his father's old age, when he himself raised the standard shortly before his father's death against Antiochus, but also from his youth had performed illustrious deeds in war, relying on God's help, and therefore had inscribed on his banners: Maccabi, that is Mi camoka baelim Jehovah, meaning 'Who is like you among the mighty, O Lord?' and from this was surnamed Maccabaeus. For thus his dying father calls him here, and therefore appoints him leader of the war. See the notes in the Proem.

And he died in the year 146, namely in the same year in which he set himself against Antiochus and undertook the leadership and High Priesthood. Some think he died from a wound received in battle, for since shortly before he had killed Apochem and the prefect of Antiochus, and had stirred up all the Jews by going about, and had gathered them into fortresses against Antiochus, and had done many similar things requiring great bodily as well as mental strength, he does not seem to have been able so quickly to fail in strength and die from old age, but rather to have received wounds from continuous battles and died therefrom, or at least to have died exhausted by incessant labors and excessive fatigue.
He left Lysia with the other half of the army and the elephants, to invade, crush, and extirpate Judas and the Jews.

And that he might rear Antiochus (surnamed Eupator) his son, a boy, in that manner and magnificence befitting the son of a king, and that, if anything should befall him as a mortal in Persia, he might substitute his son in the kingdom.

And Lysias (viceroy of Antiochus Epiphanes) chose Ptolemy son of Doryminus (in Greek Dorymenes, prefect of Coele-Syria and Phoenicia, as is clear from 2 Maccabees 8:8) and Nicanor son of Patroclus. This is that Nicanor who, sent by Lysias against Judas and the Jews, presiding and as if certain of victory, arrogantly boasted that he would sell ninety Jews as slaves for one talent, 2 Maccabees 8:11. But he paid the penalty for his boasting, when, conquered by Judas, his head was cut off and publicly hung up for mockery. And Gorgias, most experienced in military affairs, as is said there, with sixty thousand infantry and seven thousand cavalry, as follows.
And they went forth with all their force — in Greek dynamis, that is, with the power and strength of so many infantry and cavalry, together also with elephants.

And the merchants heard the forces and threats of Nicanor against the Jews, and therefore came in crowds, so that they might buy the Jews, who were to be conquered by him as they supposed, as slaves at the cheapest price. Thus at the destruction of Jerusalem by Titus, eleven hundred thousand Jews were slain and ninety-seven thousand captured and sold at a cheap price, like the most worthless slaves, as Josephus narrates in the last book on the War.

And they gathered together and came to Mizpah over against Jerusalem. This Mizpah therefore was situated on the border of the tribes of Benjamin and Judah, and thither the Jews were summoned for public assemblies and public prayer, as Samuel summoned the people there to renew the covenant of the people with God, 1 Kings 7:5. There was another Mizpah in the tribe of Gad, of which Judges 11:29; and another likewise in the tribe of Reuben, of which 1 Maccabees 5:35.

And they fasted that day until evening and the rising of the evening star after the manner of the Jews, and they put on sackcloth, and placed ashes on their heads, and rent their garments. These were acts and signs of penitence, humility, and supplication, by which, distrusting themselves in the face of so great a number of enemies and danger, they most ardently implored the help of God, and therefore obtained it. Such also were the things which follow in verses 48, 49, 50.

And they spread open the books of the Law, so that the books themselves might, as it were, cry out to the Lord with a silent voice concerning what they contained, and what they so often repeat, especially in the Psalms: Have mercy on us, O Lord, have mercy on us; and that they might move God by the display of his own books, lest he should permit them to be defiled, torn apart, and burned by the Gentiles. So Hezekiah, with Sennacherib pressing upon him, spread open the sacred books before God, that he might protect them together with his worship, 2 Kings 19.

And they brought the priestly ornaments, namely the sacred vestments, such as the mitre, the breeches, the hyacinthine tunic, the Ephod, etc., as if to say: Do not permit, O Lord, these to be seized by the Gentiles, because they themselves will tear them, or clothe with them the priests of their idols; likewise they themselves will seize and devour your first-fruits and tithes, which therefore we now display before you. The Maccabees here show themselves to be concerned not for their own things but for God's, and to be moved by zeal for his religion and worship, so as to compel God, as it were, to bring aid not so much to his people as to himself and his religion and worship. Therefore this their earnest and sincere prayer was efficacious, and bent God to come to their aid. In like manner let us also pray, and likewise bend God.

And they cried with a loud voice toward heaven, saying: What shall we do with these, and whither shall we lead them? — if Lysias captures us and takes possession of all Judea as well as Jerusalem and the temple for himself.
Gorgias took five thousand men and a thousand chosen horsemen, and they moved camp by night,
to fall upon the camp of the Jews and strike them suddenly; and the men from the citadel were their guides.
And Judas heard of it, and rose up, he and the mighty men, to attack the force of the king's army that was at Emmaus.
For the army was still dispersed from the camp.
And Gorgias came into the camp of Judas by night, and found no one, and sought them in the mountains; for he said: These men flee from us.
And when day was come, Judas appeared in the plain with three thousand men only, who had neither armor nor swords;
and they saw the camp of the Gentiles strong, and men clad in coats of mail, and horsemen round about them, and these were trained to war.
And Judas said to the men that were with him: Fear not their multitude, and be not afraid of their assault.
Remember in what manner our fathers were saved in the Red Sea, when Pharaoh pursued them with a great army.
And now let us cry to heaven, and the Lord will have mercy on us, and will remember the covenant of our fathers, and will destroy this army before our face this day.
And all nations shall know that there is one who redeemeth and delivereth Israel.
And the strangers lifted up their eyes, and saw them coming against them.
And they went out of the camp to battle, and they that were with Judas sounded the trumpet.
And they joined battle, and the Gentiles were routed, and fled into the plain.
But all the hindmost fell by the sword, and they pursued them to Gazara, and even to the plains of Idumea, and of Azotus, and of Jamnia: and there fell of them to the number of three thousand men.
And Judas returned again with his army that followed him.
And he said to the people: Be not greedy for the spoils; for there is war before us,
and Gorgias and his army are near us in the mountain: but stand ye now against our enemies, and overthrow them, and you shall take the spoils afterwards with safety.
And as Judas was speaking these words, behold part of them appeared looking down from the mountain.
And Gorgias saw that his men were put to flight, and that they had set fire to the camp; for the smoke that was seen declared what had happened.
And when they had seen these things, they were seized with great fear, seeing at the same time Judas and his army in the plain ready to fight.
And they all fled away into the land of the strangers.
And Judas returned to take the spoils of the camp, and they got much gold, and silver, and blue silk, and purple of the sea, and great riches.
And returning home, they sang a hymn, and blessed God in heaven, because he is good, because his mercy endureth for ever.
And there was a great deliverance in Israel that day.
And such of the strangers as escaped went and told Lysias all that had happened.
And when he heard these things, he was amazed and discouraged, because things had not turned out in Israel as he wished, and as the king had commanded.
And the year following Lysias gathered together threescore thousand chosen men, and five thousand horsemen, that he might subdue them.
And they came into Judea, and pitched their camp in Bethsura, and Judas met them with ten thousand men.

And he saw their army was strong, and he prayed and said: Blessed art thou, O Saviour of Israel, who didst break the violence of the mighty one by the hand of thy servant David, and didst deliver the camp of the strangers into the hands of Jonathan the son of Saul, and of his armourbearer.
Shut up this army in the hands of thy people Israel, and let them be confounded in their host and their horsemen.
Strike them with fear, and cause the boldness of their strength to languish, and let them quake at their own destruction.
Cast them down with the sword of them that love thee, and let all that know thy name praise thee with hymns.
And they joined battle: and there fell of the army of Lysias about five thousand men.

And it came to pass, when the Gentiles round about heard that the altar and the sanctuary had been built up as before, they were very angry. — Here begins chapter 5, in which Judas defeats the Ammonites, Moabites, and Idumeans, then through his brother Simon brings aid to the Galileans, and through himself to the Galaadites, and at v. 28 captures Bosor, and at v. 35 Maspha and other cities of Galaaditis. Then at v. 37 he defeats Timotheus in open battle, and at v. 46 captures Ephron; but at v. 56 Joseph and Azarias, rivals of Judas, are defeated.

And they thought to destroy the race of Jacob that was among them, and they began to kill some of the people and to persecute them. — The Gentiles who dwelt among the Jews, or near them, began to persecute and slay the Israelites living in their territories, lest they should join with Judas and turn against them.

Then Judas fought against the children of Esau in Idumea, at Acrabathane, because they besieged Israel; and he smote them with a great slaughter. — Acrabathane, or Acrabattine, was a region on the southern border of Judea toward Idumea. The children of Esau are the Idumeans, descendants of Esau, who were perpetual enemies of Israel.

And he remembered the malice of the children of Bean, who had been a snare and a stumbling-block to the people, lying in wait for them in the way. — The children of Bean, or Baean, were a tribe of Arabs or mixed people who infested the roads and ambushed travelers from among the Israelites, betraying them to their enemies.

And they were shut up by him in towers, and he encamped against them, and devoted them to destruction, and burned their towers with fire and all that were in them. — Judas surrounded them in their strongholds, besieged them, and pronounced them anathema, that is, devoted them to utter destruction according to the law of holy war (Deut. 13:15), burning both the towers and their inhabitants.

And he passed over to the children of Ammon, and he found a strong army and much people, and Timotheus was their captain. — The Ammonites, dwelling beyond the Jordan, had assembled a great force under their general Timotheus to resist Judas.

And he fought many battles with them, and they were discomfited in their sight, and he smote them. — Judas engaged them in many engagements and broke their power repeatedly, defeating Timotheus and his host.

And he took the city of Gazer and its villages, and returned into Judea. — Having conquered the Ammonite territory and taken Gazer (Jazer) with its dependent towns, Judas returned to Judea. Josephus confirms this account in his Antiquities.

And the Gentiles that were in Galaad gathered themselves together against the Israelites that were in their territories, to destroy them; and they fled to the fortress of Datheman. — The Israelites dwelling in Galaaditis, i.e., the region of Gilead beyond the Jordan, were surrounded by hostile Gentiles and took refuge in the stronghold of Datheman.

And they sent letters to Judas and his brethren, saying: The Gentiles that are around us are gathered together against us to destroy us. — The besieged Israelites of Gilead sent urgent messengers to Judas imploring his help.

And they are prepared to come and take the fortress into which we have fled, and Timotheus is the captain of their host. — The same Timotheus whom Judas had defeated among the Ammonites had now assembled a fresh army and was leading it against the Jewish refugees in Galaaditis.
Now therefore come and deliver us out of their hands, for many of us have fallen. — The letter expresses the desperate condition of the besieged, who had already suffered heavy losses.

And all our brethren that were in the places of Tubin are killed, and they have carried away their wives and their children captives, and their spoils, and they have slain there about a thousand men. — Tubin (Tob) was a district of Galaaditis. The Gentiles had massacred approximately a thousand Jewish men there and taken the women and children captive.

As these letters were yet reading, behold other messengers came from Galilee with their garments rent, bringing the like tidings. — Even while the letter from Galaaditis was being read, fresh messengers arrived from Galilee with torn garments — a sign of mourning and distress (cf. Gen. 37:29; 2 Kings 18:37) — reporting similar persecution.

Saying that they of Ptolemais, and of Tyre, and of Sidon, were assembled against them, and that all Galilee is filled with strangers, to consume us. — The Jews of Galilee were threatened by the Gentile populations of the great Phoenician coastal cities of Ptolemais (Acre), Tyre, and Sidon, who together with the pagan inhabitants of Galilee sought to annihilate them.

Now when Judas and the people heard these words, a great assembly met together to consider what they should do for their brethren that were in trouble, and were attacked by them. — A solemn assembly (Ecclesia magna) was convened, corresponding to a deliberative council of war, to determine how best to aid their beleaguered brethren simultaneously threatened in two different regions.

And Judas said to Simon his brother: Choose thee men, and go, and deliver thy brethren in Galilee; and I and my brother Jonathan will go into the country of Galaad. — Judas wisely divided his forces, sending Simon with a detachment to Galilee while he himself led the main force to the more dangerous theater of Galaaditis.

And he left Joseph the son of Zacharias, and Azarias, captains of the people, with the remnant of the army in Judea to keep it. — Judas left two subordinate commanders with a garrison force to defend Judea in his absence, entrusting its protection to them.

And he commanded them, saying: Take charge of this people, and do not engage in battle against the Gentiles till we return. — Judas gave strict orders to Joseph and Azarias not to initiate any offensive operations against the surrounding nations until his return, wishing to avoid rash engagements while the main forces were absent.

And there were given to Simon three thousand men to go into Galilee; and to Judas eight thousand men for Galaaditis. — The forces were distributed proportionally: three thousand to Simon for the Galilean campaign, and eight thousand to Judas for the more formidable expedition into Galaaditis.

And Simon went into Galilee, and fought many battles with the Gentiles, and the Gentiles were discomfited before his face, and he pursued them even to the gate of Ptolemais. — Simon pressed the enemy all the way to the walls of the great fortified city of Ptolemais (modern Acre), demonstrating the completeness of his victory.

And there fell of the Gentiles almost three thousand men, and he took the spoils of them. — Simon's campaign in Galilee resulted in the slaughter of nearly three thousand Gentile soldiers and the seizure of their plunder.

And he took with him those that were in Galilee and in Arbatis, with their wives and children, and all that they had, and he brought them into Judea with great joy. — Simon's primary mission was not conquest but the rescue of the Jewish population of Galilee and the district of Arbatis. He evacuated them with all their families and possessions to the safety of Judea, in fulfillment of his brother's command.

And Judas Machabaeus and Jonathan his brother passed over the Jordan, and went three days' journey through the desert. — To reach Galaaditis, Judas and Jonathan crossed the Jordan River and then traversed three days' journey through the wilderness, indicating the arduous nature of the expedition.

And the Nabutheans met them, and received them peacefully, and told them all that had happened to their brethren in Galaaditis. — The Nabataeans, an Arab people dwelling in the region, welcomed Judas and Jonathan in peace and served as informants, reporting the full details of the persecution of the Jews in Gilead.

And that many of them were shut up in Barasa, and Bosor, and Alimis, and Casphor, and Mageth, and Carnaim; all these strong and great cities. — The Jewish captives and refugees were confined in six fortified cities of Galaaditis: Barasa, Bosor, Alimis (Alema), Casphor, Mageth, and Carnaim (Carnion). All were large, well-fortified places.

And in the other cities of Galaaditis they are held prisoners, and they have appointed to bring their army on the morrow against those cities, and to take and destroy them all in one day. — The Gentiles had planned a coordinated simultaneous attack on all these cities the following day, intending to annihilate all the Jewish inhabitants at once.

And Judas turned, and his army, by the way of the desert of Bosor suddenly, and took the city; and he slew every male by the edge of the sword, and took all their spoils, and burned it with fire. — Judas made a sudden and unexpected night march through the wilderness toward Bosor (Bozrah), arriving before its defenders could prepare, and captured it by assault. Every male was put to the sword in accordance with the custom of holy war, the spoils were seized, and the city was burned.

And they arose from thence by night, and went till they came to the fortress. — After sacking Bosor, the army set out immediately by night without rest, marching to relieve the besieged fortress of Datheman where their brethren were trapped.

And it came to pass that when it was morning, and they lifted up their eyes, behold there was much people, whose number could not be counted, carrying ladders and engines, to take the fortress, and assail them. — At dawn Judas and his men beheld an immense besieging army equipped with scaling ladders and siege engines, already assaulting the fortress where the Israelites had taken refuge.

And Judas saw that the battle was begun, and that the cry of the battle went up to heaven like a trumpet, and a great cry out of the city. — The battle had already been joined; the tumult of the assault rose to heaven like the sound of a trumpet, and the cries of the defenders within the city showed their desperate situation.

And he said to his army: Fight ye today for your brethren. — With this brief and stirring exhortation Judas roused his men to battle on behalf of their besieged countrymen, appealing to the bond of brotherhood and the justice of their cause.

And he came with three companies behind them, and they sounded their trumpets, and cried out in prayer. — Judas attacked the besieging army from the rear with three battle-formations. The simultaneous blowing of trumpets and raising of prayer combined military tactics with religious confidence, recalling the practice of the Israelite armies of old (cf. Num. 10:9). # GEM

And the camp of Timotheus knew that it was Machabaeus, and they fled before his face; and he smote them with a great slaughter; and there fell of them in that day almost eight thousand men. — The very name of Machabaeus struck terror into the enemy. Recognizing Judas, the army of Timotheus fled in panic, and eight thousand were slain — a remarkable testimony to the reputation Judas had already acquired among his enemies.

And Judas turned aside to Maspha, and assaulted and took it; and he slew every male thereof, and took the spoils thereof, and burnt it with fire. — After the great victory, Judas pressed on to Maspha (Mizpah of Gilead), another fortified city holding Jewish captives, which he stormed and destroyed with the same thoroughness as Bosor.

From thence he marched forward, and took Casbon, and Mageth, and Bosor, and the rest of the cities of Galaaditis. — Judas continued his campaign systematically through Galaaditis, capturing in rapid succession Casbon, Mageth, Bosor (perhaps a different Bosor from the one already taken), and the remaining cities of the region, liberating the Jewish communities held captive in each.

Now after these things Timotheus gathered another army, and pitched his camp over against Raphon, beyond the torrent. — Having regrouped after his earlier defeats, Timotheus assembled a fresh army and encamped beyond a stream opposite the city of Raphon, preparing for another confrontation with Judas.

And Judas sent men to view the army; and they brought him word, saying: — Judas, acting with characteristic prudence, sent out scouts to reconnoiter the enemy's strength and dispositions before committing his forces to battle.

Eleazar, he says, by striking an elephant in battle laid it low; but he fell beneath the very beast he destroyed. Whom therefore does he signify, whom his own victory crushed, if not those who overcome vices, but succumb through pride beneath the very things they subdue? For he dies as though beneath the enemy he prostrates, who is elated by the fault he overcomes. # GEM

And seeing (Judas and the Jews) the power of the king, that is, the army of the king so strong and numerous that they themselves, being few, could not overthrow it, by prudent counsel they withdrew for a time from them, and went to besiege the citadel on mount Sion which had been occupied by the Antiochians. Likewise the king, terrified by so many of his men slain by Judas, withdrew himself, and did not dare to pursue Judas as he departed, but besieged Bethsura.
Note: In these books, by "power" (virtutem) is understood a strong army; for this is what the Greek word signifies.

And he made peace with those who were in Bethsura, with a compact that he would allow those dying of hunger to go out and seek food, while he himself would occupy Bethsura: because it was the sabbath of the land, that is, because it was the seventh, or sabbatical year, in which it was lawful neither to sow nor to reap, but rest had to be given to the land according to the law of Exodus 23. By similar reasoning Herod took Jerusalem by famine in the sabbatical year, as Josephus relates, book 14, chapter 28.
And he (king Antiochus Eupator) turned the camp toward the place of sanctification, that is, toward the temple and the citadel which Judas had erected beside it for its protection.
And to those giving aid no wheat, arms, money, ships shall be given, as it seemed good to the Romans; and they shall keep their commandments without deceit.
According to these words the Romans made a covenant with the people of the Jews.

But if after these words either party shall wish to add or take away anything from these things, they shall do so according to their own purpose; and whatsoever they shall add or take away shall be ratified.
Moreover, concerning the evils which king Demetrius did against them, we have written to him saying: Why hast thou made thy yoke heavy upon our friends and allies the Jews?
If therefore they come to us again against thee, we will do them justice and fight against thee by sea and by land.

[And Judas heard the fame (of the warlike strength and political virtues) of the Romans.] Note: The word 'heard' signifies that the heroic deeds of the Romans, which are narrated below, were brought to the ears of Judas and the Jews by hearing and report; from this, however, it does not follow that all those things are true. For report is often greater than the truth, and augments a matter beyond the truth, or distorts it, while each person adds something of his own to the report or takes away from it, as we experience daily not only in the narrations of hearers but also of eyewitnesses. This could be answered to the innovators who expunge 1 Maccabees from the canon, objecting that the author here attributes greater things to the Romans than Livy, Florus, Diodorus, and Plutarch attribute to them. So Serarius, Salianus, Sanchez and others. But if the individual things said about them here are closely examined, nothing is to be found which can be charged with falsehood or which cannot rightly be reconciled with the secular historians, as I shall show point by point. Thus in the Gospels many things are said by the Jews about Christ, such as that he was a Samaritan, a wine-drinker, possessed by a demon, which were most false; but that the Jews said these things is most true, and this alone is what Scripture intends, as St. Augustine teaches, Quaest. 6 ad Orosium.

[And they heard (Judas and the Jews) their battles AND GOOD VIRTUES,] in Greek ἀνδραγαθίαι, that is, manly deeds, acts of prowess, things bravely accomplished, acts of fortitude, illustrious exploits; for ἀνδραγαθέω means the same as to act manfully, to conduct myself as a man, to act bravely, to show myself a brave and good man.

[And what great things they did in the region of Spain (subjugating it through many long wars); AND THAT THEY BROUGHT INTO THEIR POWER THE MINES OF SILVER AND GOLD WHICH ARE THERE,] for that there were most productive mines of gold and silver in Spain at that time is attested by Pliny, Strabo, and others. That silver mines still exist there is attested by Mariana and other chroniclers and historians. See Pineda, book 4, de rebus Salomonis, ch. 11, n. 6.

[And places which were very far from them (both in Asia and in Africa, and especially Carthage the rival of Rome, etc.) they crushed.] For the wars of Rome with Hannibal and the Carthaginians conducted by Q. Fabius Maximus and P. Scipio Africanus were waged at this time, wherefore Josephus explains this verse concerning them.
[And Philip, and (his illegitimate son) Perseus (whom Livy and others call Perseus)]

And there was great tribulation in Israel, such as had not been since the day that a prophet was not seen in Israel. — Note that this tribulation was so great that it is compared to the time when prophecy ceased in Israel, namely after Malachi, the last of the prophets. For the Jews reckoned it among the greatest calamities that they were deprived of prophets, who were their guides, consolers, and defenders. So Theodoret and others note here.
And all the friends of Judas assembled together, and said to Jonathan:
Since thy brother Judas died, there is not a man like him to go forth against our enemies, Bacchides, and them that are enemies of our nation.
Now therefore we have chosen thee this day to be our prince and leader in his stead, to fight our battles.
And Jonathan took upon him the government at that time, and rose up in the place of Judas his brother.
And Bacchides had knowledge of it, and sought to kill him.
And Jonathan, and Simon his brother, and all that were with him, knew it: and they fled into the desert of Thecua, and they sat down by the water of the lake Asphar.
And Bacchides understood it, and he came himself with all his army over the Jordan on the sabbath day.
And Jonathan sent his brother, a leader of the people, and besought the Nabutheans his friends, to lend them their equipage, which was plentiful.
And the children of Jambri came out of Madaba, and took John, and all that he had, and went away with them.

After this it was told Jonathan and Simon his brother, that the children of Jambri made a great wedding, and were bringing the bride out of Madaba, the daughter of one of the great princes of Chanaan, with great pomp.
And they remembered the blood of John their brother: and they went up, and hid themselves under the covert of the mountain.

And they lifted up their eyes, and looked: and behold a tumult, and great preparation: and the bridegroom came forth, and his friends and his brethren, to meet them with timbrels, and musical instruments, and many weapons.
And they rose up against them from the place where they lay in ambush, and slew them, and many fell down slain, and the rest fled into the mountains, and they took all their spoils:
And the wedding was turned into mourning, and the voice of their musical instruments into lamentation.
And they took revenge for the blood of their brother: and they returned to the bank of the Jordan.
And Bacchides heard it, and he came on the sabbath day to the bank of the Jordan with a great power.
And Jonathan said to his company: Let us arise and fight against our enemies: for it is not now as yesterday and the day before.
For behold the battle is before us, and the water of the Jordan on this side and on that side, and banks, and marshes, and woods: and there is no place for us to turn aside.
Now therefore cry ye to heaven, that ye may be delivered from the hand of your enemies. And they joined battle.
And Jonathan stretched forth his hand to strike Bacchides, but he turned away from him backwards.
And Jonathan and they that were with him leaped into the Jordan, and swam over the Jordan to them.
And there fell of Bacchides' side that day a thousand men: and they returned to Jerusalem.
And they built strong cities in Judea, the fortress that was in Jericho, and in Ammaum, and in Bethoron, and in Bethel, and in Thamnata, and Phara, and Thopo, with high walls and gates, and bars.
And he placed garrisons in them, that they might make war upon Israel.
And he fortified the city of Bethsura, and Gazara, and the castle, and set garrisons in them, and provision of victuals.
And he took the sons of the chief men of the country for hostages, and put them in the castle in Jerusalem in custody.

Now in the year one hundred and fifty-three, in the second month, Alcimus commanded the walls of the inner court of the sanctuary to be thrown down, and the works of the prophets to be destroyed: and he began to destroy. — That is, he commanded the wall of the inner court to be demolished, which the prophets, or as some say, Haggai and Zechariah, had built after the return from Babylon. By this act Alcimus showed himself an enemy of religion, wishing to abolish the separation between Jews and Gentiles, and to open the sanctuary to all indiscriminately.
At that time Alcimus was struck: and his works were hindered, and his mouth was stopped, and he was taken with a palsy, so that he could no more speak a word, nor give order concerning his house.

And Alcimus died at that time in great torment. — A manifest judgment of God upon the sacrilegious destroyer of the holy place. So God punishes with swift vengeance those who lay hands upon sacred things. # GEM
And Bacchides saw that Alcimus was dead: and he returned to the king, and the land was quiet for two years.

And all the wicked held a consultation, saying: Behold Jonathan, and they that are with him, dwell at ease and confidently: now therefore let us bring Bacchides hither, and he shall take them all in one night.
And they went and gave him counsel.

And he arose to come with a great army: and he sent secretly letters to his confederates that were in Judea, to seize upon Jonathan and them that were with him: but they could not, for their design was known to them.
And he apprehended of the men of the country, that were the chief authors of the mischief, fifty men, and he slew them.
And Jonathan and Simon, and they that were with him, retired into Bethbessen, which is in the desert: and he repaired the breaches thereof, and they made it strong.
And Bacchides knew it, and he gathered together all his multitude: and sent word to them that were of Judea.
And he came and camped above Bethbessen, and fought against it many days, and made engines of war.
And Jonathan left Simon his brother in the city, and went forth into the country, and came with a number of men:
And he struck Odaren and his brethren, and the children of Phaseron in their tents, and he began to slay and to increase in forces.
But Simon and they that were with him, sallied out of the city, and burnt the engines of war,

And fought against Bacchides, and he was discomfited by them, and they afflicted him exceedingly. — Jonathan by his bold leadership, attacking the enemy from without while Simon pressed them from within the city, threw Bacchides into confusion and great distress.
And King Demetrius heard of it, and gathered a very large army, and went out to meet him in battle.
And Demetrius sent a letter to Jonathan with words of peace, that he might magnify him.
For he said: Let us be beforehand in making peace with him, before he makes peace with Alexander against us.
For he will remember all the evils that we have done against him, and against his brother, and against his nation.
And he gave him authority to gather an army, and to make weapons, and to be his ally; and he ordered that the hostages who were in the citadel should be delivered to him.
And Jonathan came to Jerusalem, and read the letters in the hearing of all the people, and of those who were in the citadel.
And they were seized with great fear, because they heard that the king had given him authority to gather an army.
And the hostages were delivered to Jonathan, and he restored them to their parents.
And Jonathan dwelt in Jerusalem, and began to build and restore the city.
And he directed those who were doing the work to build the walls and to surround Mount Sion with hewn stones for fortification; and they did so.
And the foreigners who were in the strongholds that Bacchides had built fled away,
and each one left his place and departed to his own land;
only in Bethsura did some of those remain who had forsaken the law and the commandments of God; for this place served as a refuge for them.

And King Alexander heard of the promises which Demetrius had made to Jonathan; and they told him of the battles and the valiant deeds which he and his brothers had performed, and of the labors which they had endured.
And he said: Shall we find such another man? And now we will make him our friend and ally.
And he wrote a letter and sent it to him according to these words, saying:
King Alexander to his brother Jonathan, greeting.
We have heard of you, that you are a man mighty in strength, and worthy to be our friend.

And now we appoint you this day to be high priest of your nation, and to be called the King's Friend (and he sent him a purple robe and a crown of gold), that you may take our part and maintain friendship with us.
And Jonathan put on the holy vestment in the seventh month of the year one hundred and sixtieth, on the feast day of Tabernacles; and he gathered an army and made many weapons.
And Demetrius heard these words and was greatly grieved, and said:
What have we done, that Alexander has forestalled us in gaining the friendship of the Jews to strengthen himself?
I also will write to them words of supplication, and offer dignities and gifts, that they may be with me in aid.
And he wrote to them in these words: King Demetrius to the nation of the Jews, greeting.
Since you have kept your covenant with us, and remained in our friendship, and have not gone over to our enemies, we have heard of it and rejoiced.
And now continue still to keep faith with us, and we will repay you with good things in return for what you have done for us;
and we will grant you many remissions, and give you gifts.
And now I free you and all the Jews from tributes, and I remit the price of salt, and I remit the crowns, and the third part of the grain

And Arsaces king of Persia and Media heard — Justin, Appian, and others call this Arsaces king of the Parthians, but the Parthians at that time ruled over Persia and Media. Orosius, book 6, chapter 4, calls this Arsaces by his proper name Mithridates, and makes him the sixth from the first Arsaces, the founder of the Parthian kingdom; for the name Arsaces was common to the kings of the Parthians, as Justin attests in book 41, who calls this Arsaces the third, with the proper name Pompatius or Priapatius, whose two sons Mithridates and Phraates afterwards both reigned among the Parthians. Now Demetrius, having been expelled from the kingdom of Syria by Tryphon, wandering through neighboring regions and seeking aid from all sides, was invited by the Greeks and Macedonians dwelling in those parts to make war on Arsaces, with great promises of assistance. Demetrius did this, and engaging with Arsaces defeated him once and then again; but at last being himself defeated and captured he came alive into the hands of Arsaces. Hear Justin, book 36, at the beginning: "The peoples of the East did not unwillingly behold the coming of Demetrius, both on account of the cruelty of Arsaces king of the Parthians, and because, accustomed to the old Macedonian empire, they bore the arrogance of this new people with indignation. And so, being aided by the auxiliaries of the Persians, Elymaeans, and Bactrians, he routed the Parthians in many battles. At last, however, deceived by a pretense of peace, he was captured, and led through the mouths of the cities, was displayed in mockery of his former favor to the peoples who had revolted. Then sent into Hyrcania, he was treated kindly and in accordance with the dignity of his former fortune."

And he put him in custody. — Demetrius having been defeated, bound, and imprisoned by Arsaces, Cleopatra, daughter of Ptolemy Philometor and wife of the now-captive Demetrius, summoned his brother Antiochus Sidetes to Seleucia, and offered him his brother's kingdom together with her own hand in marriage. Demetrius, having been freed from prison and returning to Syria with his forces, despoiled his brother Sidetes of both his kingdom and his life, as Josephus, Justin, Appian, and others attest; but of Sidetes we shall speak again at chapter 15.

And all the land was quiet (that is, Judaea and the neighboring regions of Syria and Asia) "all the days of Simon," on account of his prudent, gentle, and strong governance, such that no one dared to attack him. Wherefore from this point on the wonderful praises and deeds of Simon are recounted.

And with all his glory he took Joppa for a harbour — that is to say, Simon won great glory for himself and for all Israel, and likewise advantage and wealth, when he stormed Joppa and there established a harbour from which he might send ships to the neighboring islands, indeed throughout the whole sea, to bring in and carry out merchandise of every kind. For the harbour of Joppa is the most convenient of all Judaea; whence it endures even to this day, and accordingly all who go to Jerusalem first put in at Joppa, which is now called Jaffa. Moreover, cities through a convenient harbour become most wealthy for merchants and merchandise, as is evident in Antwerp, Venice, Lisbon, etc.
And he gathered much captivity — that is to say, Simon led away many captives from the enemy; but many Jews who had been captured by enemies he freed from captivity.
The young men put on glory — that is, they wore glorious and costly garments, as though victors triumphing over their enemies, and so they put on robes of war.
He himself restored his brothers, and drove out the enemies of Israel from them, and they established liberty for him, and described it on bronze tablets, and placed them on pillars on Mount Sion.

And this is the copy of the writing: On the eighteenth day of the month Elul, in the year one hundred and seventy-two, the third year under Simon the great priest, in Asaramel — that is, in a great assembly of priests and people and princes of the nation and elders of the region, these things were made known: Because battles have frequently been fought in our region.

But Simon the son of Mattathias, of the sons of Jarib, and his brothers gave themselves to danger, and withstood the adversaries of their nation, that their holy places and the law might stand; and they glorified their nation with great glory.
And Jonathan gathered their nation together, and became their high priest, and was gathered to his people.
And their enemies wished to tread down and destroy their region, and to stretch forth their hands against their holy things.
Then Simon resisted, and fought for his nation, and laid out much money, and armed the valiant men of his nation, and gave them wages.
And he fortified the cities of Judaea, and Bethsura which was on the borders of Judaea, where the armour of the enemies had been before, and he placed there a garrison of Jewish men.

And he fortified Joppa, which was by the sea, and Gazara, which is on the borders of Azotus, wherein the enemies dwelt before, and he placed Jews there; and whatsoever things were needful for the reformation of these he put in them.

And the people seeing the acts of Simon, and the glory which he intended to bring to his nation, made him their leader and prince of the priests, because he had done all these things, and for the justice and faith which he kept to his nation, and because he sought by all means to advance his people.

And in his days things prospered in his hands, so that the heathens were taken away out of their country, and they also that were in the city of David in Jerusalem in the castle, out of which they issued and profaned all things round about the sanctuary, and did much evil to its purity.
And he placed Jews there for the defence of the country and the city, and he raised the walls of Jerusalem.
And king Demetrius confirmed him in the high priesthood.
According to these things he made him his friend, and glorified him with great glory.
For he had heard that the Romans had called the Jews their friends and confederates and brethren, and that they had received Simon's ambassadors with honour.
And that the Jews and their priests had consented that he should be their prince and high priest for ever, till a faithful prophet should arise.

And that he should be captain over them, and that he should have charge of the sanctuary, and that he should appoint rulers over their works, and over the country, and over the armour, and over the garrisons.

And that he should have care of the holy things, and that he should be obeyed by all, and that all the writings in the country should be made in his name, and that he should be clothed with purple and gold.

And that it should not be lawful for any of the people or of the priests to disannul any of these things, or to gainsay his words, or to call together an assembly in the country without him, or to be clothed with purple, or to wear a golden buckle.
And whosoever shall do otherwise, or shall make void any of these things, shall be liable to punishment. And it pleased all the people to ordain for Simon, and to do according to these words.
And Simon accepted hereof, and was well pleased to execute the office of the high priesthood, and to be captain and prince of the nation of the Jews and of the priests, and to be chief over all.
And they said that this writing should be put on tables of brass, and that they should be set up in the circuit of the sanctuary, in a notable place.
And that a copy thereof should be put in the treasury, that Simon and his sons might have it.

King Antiochus, son of Demetrius (the elder, whose surname was Soter, and brother of Demetrius the younger, whose surname was Nicator), SENT LETTERS FROM THE ISLANDS OF THE SEA (from Rhodes, says Appian) TO SIMON THE HIGH PRIEST and prince of Judaea. That you may understand this history from the beginning, note that when Demetrius, driven into exile by Tryphon, was held captive by Arsaces, the tyrant Tryphon occupied a great part of Syria. Cleopatra, the wife of Demetrius, fled with few forces to Seleucia, to which many who detested the tyranny of Tryphon were daily defecting. She, in order to strengthen herself more in the kingdom, invited Antiochus, the younger brother of her captive husband Demetrius, to the kingdom and to marriage with her.
And he sent to him one of his friends, Athenobius, to treat with him, saying: You hold Joppa, and Gazara, and the citadel which is in Jerusalem, cities of my kingdom.
You have wasted their borders, and have made great havoc in the land, and have had dominion over many places in my kingdom.
Now therefore deliver up the cities that you have taken, and the tributes of the places whereof you have gotten dominion without the borders of Judaea.
Or else give me for them five hundred talents of silver, and for the havoc that you have made, and the tributes of the cities, other five hundred talents: or else we will come and fight against you.
So Athenobius the king's friend came to Jerusalem, and saw the glory of Simon, and the brightness in gold and silver, and his great magnificence: and he was astonished, and told him the king's words.

And Simon answered him and said: We have neither taken other men's land, nor have we possession of that which belongeth to others: but of the inheritance of our fathers, which was for some time unjustly possessed by our enemies.
But we having opportunity, hold the inheritance of our fathers.
For as to Joppe and Gazara which thou demandest, they did great harm among the people and in our country: for these we will give a hundred talents. And Athenobius answered him not a word.
But returning in anger to the king, made report to him of these words, and of the glory of Simon, and of all that he had seen: and the king was very angry.
But Tryphon fled away by ship unto Orthosias.
And the king appointed Cendebaeus captain of the sea coast, and gave him an army of footmen and horsemen.
And he commanded him to march with his army towards Judaea: and he commanded him to build up Gedeon, and to fortify the gates of the city, and to war against the people. But the king pursued Tryphon.
And Cendebaeus came to Jamnia, and began to provoke the people, and to ravage Judaea, and to take the people prisoners, and to kill, and to build Gedeon.
And he placed there horsemen and an army, that they might issue forth and make incursions upon the ways of Judaea, as the king had commanded him.
AND JOHN WENT UP FROM GAZARA, that is, from Gaza where Simon his father had left and placed him in his stead as a kind of commander of the army.
(no additional comment beyond verse 1 note)

NOW I AM OLD; BUT BE YE INSTEAD OF ME, AND MY BROTHER'S. The Greek and Latin Complutensian have: and of my brother, namely of Jonathan and Judas, that you may succeed us in the leadership and wars for Israel. The Greek adds: But you are of fitting age by the benefit of years, that is, you are fit for war by the gift of age.

AND HE CHOSE FROM THE REGION TWENTY THOUSAND MEN OF WAR (warriors) AND HORSEMEN. Here only do I find cavalry in the camp of John and the Jews, whereas in the camps of Judas, Jonathan, and Simon up to this point we have read of infantry only, because God in Deuteronomy 17 had forbidden the Jews the multiplication of horses.

AND HE CROSSED OVER FIRST, namely John himself the son of Simon and commander of the army, whose example the others, who had previously trembled, immediately followed; wherefore attacking Cendebaeus boldly they struck him down and put him to flight.

UNTIL HE CAME TO CEDRON (that is, to Gedom, for this is called Cedron in Greek; this therefore John wrested from the fleeing enemy by pressing upon them) WHICH HE HAD BUILT, that is, which Cendebaeus had built and fortified, preceding chapter v. 39. This was the beginning of the victories and heroic deeds of John with which he himself bravely and successfully entered upon the leadership in place of Simon his father, for whom death was already being prepared by the treachery of Ptolemy. See here the providence of God which provided for Israel through John, while Simon his father and leader was being killed by the fraud of his son-in-law. Furthermore Josephus embellishes this battle after his manner, and therefore mixes in many false things, namely that Simon himself was present at this battle, that he attacked Cendebaeus by ambushes, that he fought with him several times and always came off victor.

AND PTOLEMY THE SON OF ABOBUS WAS APPOINTED COMMANDER IN THE PLAIN OF JERICHO, namely by Simon the Duke and High Priest his father-in-law, as follows. This Ptolemy was wealthy, and through his riches he aimed to obtain the leadership of Judaea; and therefore he planned to destroy Simon with his sons and the whole stock of the Asmonaeans. See here the monstrous parricide and sacrilege of Ptolemy, who by fraud killed the High Priest whose daughter he himself had taken as wife. What will ambition and the lust for power not dare?
FOR HE WAS SON-IN-LAW OF THE HIGH PRIEST.
AND HIS HEART WAS LIFTED UP, AND HE WANTED TO OBTAIN THE REGION, AND PLOTTED TREACHERY AGAINST SIMON AND HIS SONS, TO DESTROY THEM.

AND SIMON, GOING ABOUT THE CITIES THAT WERE IN THE REGION OF JUDAEA AND TAKING CARE OF THEM, WENT DOWN TO JERICHO, HE AND MATHATHIAS HIS SON AND JUDAS, IN THE HUNDRED AND SEVENTY-SEVENTH YEAR, IN THE ELEVENTH MONTH, WHICH IS THE MONTH SABATH.
AND THE SON OF ABOBUS RECEIVED THEM INTO A LITTLE STRONGHOLD CALLED DOCH, WHICH HE HAD BUILT, WITH TREACHERY; AND HE MADE THEM A GREAT BANQUET, AND HID MEN THERE.

AND WHEN SIMON WAS DRUNK (that is, made merry by rather liberal drinking) — without true drunkenness understood — AND HIS SONS, PTOLEMY AROSE WITH HIS MEN, AND THEY TOOK THEIR WEAPONS AND ENTERED THE BANQUET, AND KILLED HIM AND HIS TWO SONS AND CERTAIN OF HIS SERVANTS.
AND HE COMMITTED GREAT TREACHERY IN ISRAEL, AND RETURNED EVIL FOR GOOD.
AND PTOLEMY WROTE THESE THINGS AND SENT TO THE KING THAT HE SHOULD SEND HIM AN ARMY FOR HELP AND DELIVER TO HIM THE REGION AND THE CITIES AND THE TRIBUTES.
AND HE SENT OTHERS TO GAZARA TO KILL JOHN; AND TO THE TRIBUNES HE SENT LETTERS THAT THEY SHOULD COME TO HIM, AND HE WOULD GIVE THEM SILVER AND GOLD AND GIFTS.
AND HE SENT OTHERS TO TAKE JERUSALEM AND THE MOUNTAIN OF THE TEMPLE.
AND ONE RUNNING BEFORE TOLD JOHN IN GAZARA THAT HIS FATHER AND HIS BROTHERS HAD PERISHED, AND THAT HE HAD SENT TO KILL HIM ALSO.
AND WHEN HE HEARD IT HE WAS GREATLY AFRAID; AND HE SEIZED THE MEN WHO CAME TO DESTROY HIM AND KILLED THEM; FOR HE KNEW THAT THEY SOUGHT TO DESTROY HIM.
AND THE REST OF THE ACTS OF JOHN AND HIS WARS AND HIS GOOD DEEDS IN WHICH HE ACTED BRAVELY, AND THE BUILDING OF THE WALLS WHICH HE CONSTRUCTED, AND HIS ACHIEVEMENTS.
BEHOLD THESE ARE WRITTEN IN THE BOOK OF THE DAYS OF HIS PRIESTHOOD, FROM THE TIME HE BECAME HIGH PRIEST AFTER HIS FATHER.
